Some thoughts on what I've learned from managing field operations — and how it applies to everything...

![Construction blueprints on a desk](https://picsum.photos/seed/building/800/350)

There's something deeply satisfying about watching a system you built actually work in the real world.

I've spent years working in **field operations** — coordinating deployments, managing teams across multiple sites, and making sure things happen on time and on budget. It's not glamorous work, but it's taught me a lot about what makes things tick.

## Lessons from the field

**1. Plans are worthless, but planning is everything.**
No matter how detailed your deployment plan is, something will go wrong on the ground. The value isn't in the plan itself — it's in the thinking you did to create it.

**2. Communication is the real infrastructure.**
You can have the best logistics in the world, but if your team doesn't know what's happening, none of it matters. I've learned to over-communicate, especially across time zones and languages.

**3. Small teams outperform big ones.**
Give me 5 people who care over 20 who don't. Every time.

## What this means for my work

These days, I bring this operational mindset to everything I do. Whether it's managing a POSM deployment or building this very website, the principles are the same: **plan thoroughly, communicate clearly, and iterate constantly.**

Why I built my site like an email client
A quick behind-the-scenes look at why this site looks like Thunderbird and how I built it with Astro...

![Email client interface on a monitor](https://picsum.photos/seed/emailclient/800/350)

You might be wondering — why does this site look like an email client?

Good question. Here's the thing: I spend a lot of my day in email. It's where conversations happen, where deals are made, and where work gets done. So when I decided to rebuild my personal site, I wanted it to feel familiar and functional.

## The inspiration

I've been a **Mozilla Thunderbird** user for years. There's something about its three-panel layout — sidebar, message list, reading pane — that just makes sense for browsing content. It's information-dense without feeling overwhelming.

## The tech stack

This site is built with [Astro](https://astro.build), a modern static site generator that's perfect for content-focused websites. Here's what I used:

- **Astro** — Static site generation with content collections
- **Vanilla CSS** — No framework, just custom properties and thoughtful design tokens
- **SVG icons** — Inline SVGs for crisp, lightweight icons
- **Google Fonts (Inter)** — Clean, modern typography

The entire site is static HTML — no JavaScript frameworks, no client-side rendering. It's fast, accessible, and works everywhere.

## The design philosophy

The key was getting the details right:
- Dark sidebar with purple accents (just like Thunderbird)
- Light reading pane for comfortable reading
- Message list with sender avatars, dates, and previews
- Action buttons that look interactive (even if decorative)

It's form meeting function — a portfolio that feels like a tool you already know how to use.

Hey! Welcome to my personal corner of the internet. This site is built to look and feel like an email client...

Hey there!

Welcome to my personal corner of the internet. I'm **Adi Setyawan** — a creative professional who's passionate about operations, field team management, and making things run smoothly.

This site is a little different from your typical portfolio. I built it to look and feel like your email client — because in today's world, email is where the magic happens. Job offers, project updates, creative briefs — it all starts with an email.

## How to navigate

Think of this site like your inbox:

- **Inbox** — You're here! This is where you'll find my latest thoughts, blog posts, and updates
- **Sent** — My projects and work I've shipped out into the world
- **Drafts** — Things I'm currently working on
- **Notes** — Learn more about me
- **Archive** — Older projects and past work
- **Starred** — My featured and pinned content

Feel free to click around and explore. And if you'd like to reach out, hit that **"Send Message"** button!

The design system behind this site
A deep dive into the Frutiger Aero-inspired design tokens, color palette, and component architecture...

Every detail of this site is built on a small but intentional design system. Here's how it all fits together.

![Design system color palette](https://picsum.photos/seed/colors/800/350)

## Color Palette

The design draws from the **Frutiger Aero** aesthetic — soft gradients, glassy surfaces, and blue-sky warmth. Here's the core palette:

| Token | Value | Usage |
|-------|-------|-------|
| `--accent` | `#0ea5e9` | Links, active states, buttons |
| `--bg-sidebar` | `#1e293b` | Sidebar background |
| `--bg-list` | `#ffffff` | Message list background |
| `--bg-detail` | `#fafbfc` | Reading pane background |
| `--text-primary` | `#0f172a` | Headings, strong text |
| `--text-detail` | `#334155` | Body text |
| `--text-muted` | `#94a3b8` | Dates, secondary info |
| `--border-subtle` | `#e2e8f0` | Dividers, card borders |

## Typography Scale

The site uses **Inter** as the primary typeface across all elements:

| Token | Size | Usage |
|-------|------|-------|
| `--font-size-xs` | 11px | Timestamps, labels |
| `--font-size-sm` | 13px | Previews, meta text |
| `--font-size-base` | 14px | Sidebar items |
| `--font-size-md` | 15px | Body text |
| `--font-size-lg` | 18px | Section headings |
| `--font-size-xl` | 22px | Page titles |

## Spacing System

Consistent spacing creates visual rhythm:

1. **4px** (`--spacing-xs`) — Tight gaps between related elements
2. **8px** (`--spacing-sm`) — Standard component padding
3. **12px** (`--spacing-md`) — Card internal spacing
4. **16px** (`--spacing-lg`) — Section gaps
5. **24px** (`--spacing-xl`) — Major section padding
6. **32px** (`--spacing-2xl`) — Page-level margins

## Component Architecture

The site is built with Astro components that compose together:

```
EmailLayout.astro
├── Toolbar.astro
├── Sidebar.astro
├── MessageList.astro (folder views)
└── ReadingPane.astro (content view)
    ├── Action bar (reply, forward, nav)
    ├── Post meta (from, to, date)
    ├── Email body (markdown content)
    ├── Signature (auto-generated)
    └── Reply modal (Formspree)
```

![Component architecture diagram](https://picsum.photos/seed/architecture/800/400)

## Responsive Breakpoints

The layout adapts across screen sizes:

| Breakpoint | Behavior |
|------------|----------|
| Desktop (1024px+) | Full three-panel layout |
| Tablet (768px–1023px) | Sidebar collapses, two panels |
| Mobile (< 768px) | Single panel, hamburger menu |

## Design Principles

Three rules guide every design decision:

1. **Familiarity over novelty** — The email client metaphor works because people already know how to use it. Don't fight that.
2. **Content density matters** — Show enough information to be useful without overwhelming. The message list preview is exactly two lines for a reason.
3. **Subtle depth** — Soft shadows, gentle gradients, and layered backgrounds create a sense of space without being heavy-handed.

> The best interface is one you already know how to use.

I'm writing to express my interest in the Operations Manager position at Displaymax (M) Sdn Bhd in Selangor. Based on the job description, I feel this is a great match for the kind of work I've been doing and exactly where I hope to grow further.

I've handled full-cycle field operations such as **planning, scheduling, deploying teams, managing inventory**, and ensuring day-to-day activities run smoothly on the ground. I'm comfortable leading cross functional teams, resolving issues on site, and maintaining clear communication with both internal stakeholders and clients.

POSM Deployment Tracker — Field Operations Tool
A spreadsheet-based tracking system for managing POSM deployments across 20+ retail locations...

This project was built to solve a real problem: tracking Point of Sale Materials deployments across dozens of retail locations with no budget for enterprise software.

![Deployment tracking dashboard](https://picsum.photos/seed/tracker/800/400)

## The Problem

Managing POSM deployments across 20+ sites meant tracking hundreds of individual items. The existing process was:

1. Receive deployment schedule via email
2. Manually assign teams to locations
3. Track progress through WhatsApp group chats
4. Compile completion reports by hand at end of day

This led to missed deadlines, double-deployments, and reports that were always a day behind.

## The Solution

A Google Sheets-based tracking system with automated reporting.

### System Architecture

| Component | Tool | Purpose |
|-----------|------|---------|
| Data entry | Google Sheets | Field teams log completion |
| Automation | Google Apps Script | Auto-generates daily reports |
| Dashboard | Google Data Studio | Visual status overview |
| Alerts | Email triggers | Notifies for overdue tasks |

### Deployment Status Codes

| Code | Status | Action Required |
|------|--------|-----------------|
| `P` | Pending | Not yet started |
| `IP` | In Progress | Team on-site |
| `C` | Completed | Verified and documented |
| `X` | Blocked | Issue needs resolution |
| `S` | Skipped | Rescheduled for later |

## Key Features

The tracker included several features that made field work smoother:

- **Real-time status** — Field teams update directly from their phones
- **Auto-generated daily reports** — No more manual compilation
- **Overdue alerts** — Automatic email when tasks pass deadline
- **Photo documentation** — Teams upload completion photos per site
- **Historical data** — Track trends across deployment cycles

![Field team using the tracker on mobile](https://picsum.photos/seed/mobile-tracker/800/450)

## Results

After three months of use:

| Metric | Before | After | Improvement |
|--------|--------|-------|-------------|
| Report delivery time | Next day | Same day | -24 hours |
| Deployment accuracy | 82% | 96% | +14% |
| Material waste | ~15% | ~5% | -10% |
| Manager oversight time | 3 hrs/day | 45 min/day | -75% |

## Lessons Learned

1. **Build for the user, not the manager** — The field teams needed something simple on their phones, not a complex dashboard
2. **Google Sheets is underrated** — For small teams without IT budget, Sheets + Apps Script is incredibly powerful
3. **Automate the boring parts** — Nobody should spend hours compiling reports that a script can generate in seconds

> The best tool is the one your team will actually use.

Built a Thunderbird-inspired personal site using Astro, vanilla CSS, and content collections...

![Code editor with a web project open](https://picsum.photos/seed/portfolio/800/350)

Hi there,

I wanted to share my latest project: **setyawanadi.design** — the very site you're browsing right now!

## Project Overview

A personal portfolio and blog built to look and feel like Mozilla Thunderbird, the email client. The concept is simple: your personal content (blog posts, projects, about page) is presented as "emails" in a familiar three-panel layout.

## Tech Stack

| Technology | Purpose |
|-----------|---------|
| **Astro** | Static site generation |
| **Vanilla CSS** | Custom design system |
| **Content Collections** | Typed content management |
| **SVG Icons** | Lightweight, crisp icons |
| **Inter Font** | Modern typography |

## Design Features

- **Three-panel layout** — Sidebar, message list, reading pane
- **Dark theme sidebar** — Purple accents inspired by Thunderbird
- **Light reading pane** — Optimized for content readability
- **Responsive design** — Adapts to mobile and tablet
- **Email metaphor** — Folders map to content sections

## What I Learned

Building this project taught me a lot about CSS Grid layouts, Astro's content collections system, and the art of making a novelty concept feel genuinely usable.

Documentation of my previous role coordinating field operations across retail sites...

![Warehouse logistics operations](https://picsum.photos/seed/fieldops/800/350)

## Role Summary

**Field Operations Coordinator** — coordinating multi-site retail deployments and managing on-the-ground teams for POSM installations and promotional setups.

## Key Responsibilities

- Coordinated deployment schedules for teams across **15+ retail locations**
- Managed inventory logistics including **receiving, cataloging, and distributing** promotional materials
- Maintained daily communication with site managers, vendors, and headquarters
- Generated weekly operational reports tracking **completion rates, issues, and resource utilization**
- Troubleshot on-site problems including shipping delays, material shortages, and staffing issues

## Achievements

- Improved deployment completion rate from 78% to **94%** through better scheduling
- Reduced material waste by **30%** through improved inventory tracking
- Developed a spreadsheet-based reporting system that cut report generation time by **50%**

## Lessons Learned

This role taught me that operations success comes down to three things: **preparation, communication, and adaptability**. No amount of planning can account for every variable on the ground, but thorough preparation gives you the foundation to handle surprises.

Working on a new workflow automation system to streamline field operations reporting...

*This is a draft — work in progress.*

## The idea

I've been thinking about how to automate some of the repetitive reporting tasks in field operations. Right now, a lot of the work involves:

- Manually compiling daily activity reports
- Cross-referencing inventory counts across sites
- Tracking team deployment schedules in spreadsheets

## What I'm building

A simple dashboard that pulls data from multiple sources and generates daily reports automatically. The stack I'm considering:

- **Google Sheets API** for data input (teams are already comfortable with Sheets)
- **Node.js** backend for data processing
- **Simple web dashboard** for visualization

*More details coming soon...*

Hi! I'm Adi Setyawan — an operations professional with a passion for field team management...

Hi!

I'm **Adi Setyawan**, an operations professional based in Southeast Asia with a passion for field team management, logistics coordination, and making systems work better.

## What I do

I specialize in **full-cycle field operations** — from planning and scheduling to on-the-ground execution. My experience includes:

- **Team deployment & coordination** across multiple sites
- **Inventory management** and logistics planning
- **POSM (Point of Sale Materials) deployment** for retail and marketing campaigns
- **Operational reporting** and performance tracking
- **Cross-functional communication** between stakeholders, vendors, and field teams

## My approach

I believe in three things:

1. **Clarity over complexity** — Simple systems that everyone understands beat sophisticated ones that nobody uses
2. **Hands-on leadership** — The best managers are the ones who know what the work actually looks like on the ground
3. **Continuous improvement** — There's always a better way to do things, and I'm always looking for it

## Beyond work

When I'm not managing operations, you'll find me tinkering with web technologies (like this site!), exploring new tools, and learning about design systems. I'm also a Mozilla Thunderbird enthusiast — which is probably obvious from the look of this site.

## Get in touch

I'm always open to interesting conversations and new opportunities.

- **Email:** [setyawanadi@hotmail.com](mailto:setyawanadi@hotmail.com)

Hi!

I’m Adi Setyawan, an operations professional based in Southeast Asia with a passion for field team management, logistics coordination, and making systems work better.

What I do

I specialize in full-cycle field operations — from planning and scheduling to on-the-ground execution. My experience includes:

  • Team deployment & coordination across multiple sites
  • Inventory management and logistics planning
  • POSM (Point of Sale Materials) deployment for retail and marketing campaigns
  • Operational reporting and performance tracking
  • Cross-functional communication between stakeholders, vendors, and field teams

My approach

I believe in three things:

  1. Clarity over complexity — Simple systems that everyone understands beat sophisticated ones that nobody uses
  2. Hands-on leadership — The best managers are the ones who know what the work actually looks like on the ground
  3. Continuous improvement — There’s always a better way to do things, and I’m always looking for it

Beyond work

When I’m not managing operations, you’ll find me tinkering with web technologies (like this site!), exploring new tools, and learning about design systems. I’m also a Mozilla Thunderbird enthusiast — which is probably obvious from the look of this site.

Get in touch

I’m always open to interesting conversations and new opportunities.
